What is the creation mandate / cultural mandate?

The Creation Mandate, also known as the Cultural Mandate or Dominion Mandate, is God's foundational command to humanity in Genesis 1:28, given to Adam and Eve before the fall: "And God blessed them. And God said to them, 'Be fruitful and multiply and fill the earth and subdue it, and have dominion over the fish of the sea and over the birds of the heavens and over every living thing that moves on the earth'" (Genesis 1:28, English Standard Version).

This mandate has several key elements:
- **Be fruitful and multiply, and fill the earth**: A call to procreation, family, and populating the world.
- **Subdue it and have dominion**: An invitation to steward and cultivate creation responsibly, exercising wise rule over the earth and its creatures as God's image-bearers (Genesis 1:26-27). This is echoed in Genesis 2:15, where Adam is placed in the garden "to work it and keep it."

Though sin disrupted perfect harmony with creation (Genesis 3), the mandate endures as part of humanity's ongoing purpose under God's sovereignty. It undergirds vocations like agriculture, science, art, technology, and environmental care—all as acts of worship and service. In the New Testament, Jesus' Great Commission in Matthew 28:18-20 extends this by calling disciples to fill the earth with the Gospel while stewarding all nations under Christ's lordship.

As believers, we're invited to pursue this faithfully, resting in God's grace for strength (Colossians 3:23). How might this shape your daily work or calling?
What does it mean that friendship with the world is enmity with God (James 4:4)?

James 4:4 states, "You adulterous people! Do you not know that friendship with the world is enmity with God? Therefore whoever wishes to be a friend of the world makes himself an enemy of God" (ESV).

This verse warns believers against spiritual unfaithfulness, using the imagery of adultery to describe divided loyalties. "The world" here refers not to God's creation or lost people, but to the ungodly system of values, desires, and influences that oppose Him—things like pride, selfish ambition, sensual pleasures, and conformity to sinful cultural pressures (see 1 John 2:15-17: "Do not love the world or the things in the world... For all that is in the world—the desires of the flesh and the desires of the eyes and pride of life—is not from the Father but is from the world").

"Friendship with the world" means embracing those values, seeking the world's approval over God's, and living as if its priorities align with His kingdom. This creates "enmity with God"—active hostility or opposition—because no one can serve two masters (Matthew 6:24). God is a jealous lover (James 4:5), and such compromise is like marital infidelity against Him.

In context, James 4 urges humility, submission to God, and resistance to the devil amid quarrels and cravings (James 4:1-10). The good news is repentance leads to restoration: "Draw near to God, and he will draw near to you" (James 4:8). As believers, we're called to be in the world but not of it (John 17:14-16; Romans 12:2), transformed by His grace to love God wholeheartedly while showing compassion to others. If worldly pulls tempt you, confess it to Him—He is faithful to cleanse and renew (1 John 1:9).
Why did God harden Pharaoh’s heart?

God hardened Pharaoh's heart as an act of sovereign judgment on Pharaoh's prior rebellion, using it to accomplish His greater purposes in delivering Israel and revealing His power to the nations. Scripture shows a progression: Pharaoh first hardens his own heart multiple times, rejecting God's clear signs and commands (Exodus 8:15; Exodus 8:32; Exodus 9:34). Only after persistent defiance does God confirm this hardness (Exodus 4:21; Exodus 7:3; Exodus 9:12; Exodus 10:1; Exodus 11:10), much like a judge who, after repeated warnings, hands over a criminal to face the full consequences of their choices (Romans 1:24-28).

The explicit purpose is stated in Exodus 9:16: God raised Pharaoh up "so that My name may be proclaimed in all the earth," using the plagues to display His supreme power over Egypt's false gods and to free His people (Exodus 7:5; Exodus 14:4). Paul later explains this in Romans 9:17-18, quoting Exodus 9:16 and affirming, "So then He has mercy on whom He desires, and He hardens whom He desires," underscoring God's right as Creator to show justice and mercy as He wills, without injustice (Romans 9:14).

Importantly, this reveals God's holiness and justice against idolatry and oppression—Pharaoh enslaved God's people cruelly—while highlighting His patience in sending Moses with multiple chances to repent (Exodus 10:3). It points forward to the Gospel, where God hardens some hearts in judgment but offers mercy through Christ to all who repent and believe (Romans 9:22-24). If Pharaoh's story raises questions for you about God's fairness or your own life, what aspect stands out most?
How should we treat our enemies according to Proverbs 25:21–22?

Proverbs 25:21-22 instructs us to respond to our enemies with unexpected kindness: "If your enemy is hungry, give him food to eat; and if he is thirsty, give him water to drink. In doing this, you will heap burning coals on his head, and the Lord will reward you."

This proverb calls believers to practical generosity—meeting tangible needs like hunger and thirst—rather than retaliation. The "burning coals" likely symbolize either deep shame that prompts repentance (as in ancient Egyptian rituals of remorse) or the intense conviction of God's judgment, but the focus is on our merciful action mirroring God's grace toward us.

Jesus echoes and expands this in the Sermon on the Mount: "Love your enemies and pray for those who persecute you" (Matthew 5:44). Paul quotes it directly in Romans 12:20 amid commands to overcome evil with good, urging us not to be conquered by evil but to conquer it with kindness (Romans 12:21).

In practice, treat enemies as neighbors in need: offer help without strings, trusting God for outcomes and rewards. This reflects Christ's love for us while we were His enemies (Romans 5:8-10), disarming hatred and displaying the gospel's power. May the Lord give you grace to live this out today.
What does the Bible say about indecision / being indecisive?

The Bible addresses indecision as a heart issue often rooted in doubt, divided loyalties, or lack of trust in God, portraying it as unstable and hindering fruitfulness. James warns that the double-minded person, unstable in all their ways, should not expect to receive anything from the Lord, but urges believers lacking wisdom to ask God generously, who gives without finding fault (James 1:5-8). This double-mindedness reflects hesitation between trusting God and leaning on our own understanding.

Proverbs echoes this, calling us to trust in the Lord with all our heart and not lean on our own understanding; in all our ways acknowledge Him, and He will make our paths straight (Proverbs 3:5-6). Indecision is like limping between two opinions, as Elijah challenged Israel: "How long will you waver between two opinions? If the Lord is God, follow him" (1 Kings 18:21). Joshua similarly pressed for decision: "Choose for yourselves this day whom you will serve... But as for me and my household, we will serve the Lord" (Joshua 24:15).

Jesus taught no one can serve two masters; we'll either hate the one and love the other (Luke 16:13). The remedy is single-minded pursuit of God: fix your gaze straight ahead, avoid turning right or left (Proverbs 4:25-27).

Practically, pray for wisdom as James instructs, immerse yourself in Scripture, seek godly counsel (Proverbs 11:14), and step forward in faith, knowing God directs the humble (Psalm 25:9). He delights in guiding His children—rest in that promise today. If a specific decision weighs on you, what is it? I'd be glad to pray with you toward Scripture's light.
What does it mean that God will wipe away every tear (Revelation 21:4)?

Revelation 21:4 describes a glorious future promise from God in the vision of the new heaven and new earth: "He will wipe every tear from their eyes, and death shall be no more, neither shall there be mourning, nor crying, nor pain anymore, for the former things have passed away." This is part of John's prophetic glimpse into eternity after Christ's victorious return, the final judgment, and the renewal of all creation (Revelation 21:1-5).

The imagery of God personally "wiping away every tear" signifies His intimate, tender care for His redeemed people. In this perfected state, every source of sorrow—stemming from sin, death, brokenness, and suffering—will be completely eradicated. No more pain, loss, or grief; instead, unending joy in the direct presence of God, who dwells fully with humanity (Revelation 21:3). This echoes Isaiah's prophecy: "He will swallow up death forever; and the Lord God will wipe away tears from all faces" (Isaiah 25:8).

For believers today, this verse offers profound hope amid trials, assuring us that our present sufferings are temporary and that God is making all things new (2 Corinthians 4:17-18; Romans 8:18). It invites us to trust His faithfulness, looking forward to the day when He comforts us eternally. What aspect of this promise resonates most with you right now?
What is the meaning of “there but for the grace of God go I”?

The phrase "there but for the grace of God go I" is a humble acknowledgment of human frailty and God's sovereign mercy. It means that without God's preventing grace, any of us could easily fall into the same sin, suffering, or downfall we observe in others. It's a reminder that our moral standing, victories over temptation, or spared circumstances are not due to our own strength or goodness, but solely to God's unmerited favor keeping us from ruin.

This sentiment echoes the Bible's teaching on universal sinfulness—"for all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God" (Romans 3:23)—and salvation by grace: "For by grace you have been saved through faith. And this is not your own doing; it is the gift of God, not a result of works, so that no one may boast" (Ephesians 2:8-9). Often attributed to the 16th-century English reformer John Bradford, who reportedly said it upon seeing criminals led to execution, it fosters compassion rather than judgment, urging us to respond to others' struggles with empathy, prayer, and gospel hope.

In practice, when we see someone's failure, it invites self-examination: "Who am I to condemn, when only God's grace sustains me?" (see 1 Corinthians 10:12-13). How might this truth shape your view of a particular situation?
Who wrote the book of Hosea? Who was the author of Hosea?

The prophet Hosea, son of Beeri, is the author of the book of Hosea. This is indicated at the beginning of the book: "The word of the Lord that came to Hosea, the son of Beeri" (Hosea 1:1). He ministered in the northern kingdom of Israel during the reigns of several kings, around the 8th century BC, delivering God's message through his life and prophecies.
What were the Holy Place and the Most Holy Place in the temple / tabernacle?

The tabernacle, described in Exodus 25–40, was the portable dwelling place of God among the Israelites during their wilderness journey, later replicated in a more permanent form in Solomon's temple (1 Kings 6) and subsequent temples. It was divided into two main chambers by a thick veil: the Holy Place and the Most Holy Place (also called the Holy of Holies).

### The Holy Place
This was the larger outer chamber, accessible only to priests. It contained three key furnishings symbolizing God's provision and presence:
- The golden lampstand (menorah), which provided perpetual light (Exodus 25:31–40; 27:20–21).
- The table of showbread, holding twelve loaves representing Israel's twelve tribes and God's sustaining grace (Exodus 25:23–30).
- The altar of incense, where priests burned fragrant offerings symbolizing prayer ascending to God (Exodus 30:1–10).

Priests entered here daily to perform worship duties, such as trimming the lamps, changing the bread weekly, and offering incense morning and evening (Exodus 30:7–8; Leviticus 24:5–9). It represented the realm of God's fellowship with His covenant people through mediated access.

### The Most Holy Place
This smaller inner chamber, separated by the veil (Exodus 26:31–33), housed the Ark of the Covenant—a gold-covered chest containing the tablets of the Ten Commandments, Aaron's rod, and a jar of manna (Exodus 25:10–22; Hebrews 9:4). Atop the ark was the mercy seat, where God's presence dwelled between the cherubim, signifying atonement (Leviticus 16:2).

Only the high priest could enter, and only once a year on the Day of Atonement (Yom Kippur), sprinkling blood on the mercy seat to atone for Israel's sins (Leviticus 16:11–17; Hebrews 9:7). The veil symbolized the barrier of sin separating sinful humanity from God's perfect holiness (Isaiah 59:2).

In the New Testament, these foreshadow Christ's work: His body was the true temple (John 2:19–21), His blood the final atonement (Hebrews 9:11–14), and at His death, the temple veil tore, granting all believers direct access to God by faith (Matthew 27:51; Hebrews 10:19–22). What a beautiful picture of God's pursuing grace!
How can we walk in God’s ways (Psalm 128:1)?

Blessed is everyone who fears the Lord and walks in his ways, as Psalm 128:1 declares. Walking in God's ways means living in loving obedience to His commands, rooted in reverent awe of Him (Proverbs 1:7; Deuteronomy 10:12-13). This isn't mere rule-keeping but a joyful response to His grace, empowered by the Holy Spirit after trusting in Christ's atoning death and resurrection.

Here are biblical ways to pursue this path:

- **Delight in and meditate on Scripture daily**, for it lights our steps and renews our minds (Psalm 119:105; Psalm 1:1-3; Joshua 1:8). Start with Psalms or the Gospels, asking God to reveal His ways.
  
- **Pray continually**, confessing sin, seeking His will, and thanking Him, as prayer aligns our hearts with His (1 Thessalonians 5:17; Colossians 4:2; Psalm 86:11).

- **Repent and rely on the Spirit**, turning from sin to Christ moment by moment; He produces fruit like love, joy, and self-control (Galatians 5:16-25; Romans 8:13-14).

- **Love others as Christ loved us**, serving humbly in family, church, and community—this fulfills His law (John 13:34-35; Micah 6:8; Ephesians 5:1-2).

- **Fellowship with believers**, submitting to godly leaders and spurring one another toward love and good deeds (Hebrews 10:24-25; Hebrews 13:17).

God promises blessing—not always ease, but fruitfulness and peace (Psalm 128:2-6; Matthew 6:33). He who began this good work in you will complete it (Philippians 1:6). Take one step today, trusting His strength, and watch Him guide your path (Proverbs 3:5-6). What area feels most pressing for you right now?
What does the Bible say about the ice age?

The Bible does not explicitly mention an "ice age" as a geological period known in modern science. However, it poetically describes God's sovereign control over cold, snow, ice, and frost, emphasizing His power in creation and weather.

For example, Job 38:29-30 records God asking Job: "Out of whose womb came the ice? and the hoary frost of heaven, who hath gendered it? The waters are hid as with a stone, and the face of the deep is frozen." Similarly, Psalm 147:16-17 says, "He giveth snow like wool: he scattereth the hoarfrost like ashes. He casteth forth his ice like morsels: who can stand before his cold?"

Some Christians, particularly young-earth creationists, connect these themes to a possible post-Flood ice age, suggesting rapid climate shifts after Noah's Flood (Genesis 6-9) due to warmer oceans, volcanic cooling, and shifting landmasses. Others hold different views on earth's history, seeing room for longer timelines while affirming God's creation (Genesis 1:1).

Scripture prioritizes God's majesty over weather details (Job 37:5-13; Psalm 148:8), inviting us to trust His wisdom amid scientific questions. If you're exploring this, prayerfully study Genesis alongside trusted resources from faithful scholars, and consider discussing with your church leaders. What aspect intrigues you most?
What is the significance of the city of Nineveh in the Bible?

Nineveh holds profound significance in the Bible as the capital of the Assyrian Empire, a powerful and often brutal enemy of Israel and Judah. It first appears in Genesis 10:11-12 as a city founded by Nimrod, symbolizing human expansion and empire-building apart from God.

Its most prominent role is in the Book of Jonah, where God commissions Jonah to preach against Nineveh "because their wickedness has come up before me" (Jonah 1:2). The Ninevites, from king to commoner, respond with genuine repentance—fasting, wearing sackcloth, and turning from evil—and God relents from destroying the city (Jonah 3:5-10). This story dramatically illustrates God's mercy extending beyond Israel to Gentiles, His compassion for repentant sinners (Jonah 4:11), and the universal scope of His judgment and grace. It challenges Jonah's (and our) narrow views of who deserves God's love.

Later prophets foretell Nineveh's ultimate downfall due to persistent violence and idolatry. The prophet Nahum delivers an entire oracle of judgment against it (Nahum 1:1–3:19), fulfilled historically when Babylonians and Medes destroyed the city around 612 BC. Zephaniah 2:13 and others echo this doom, underscoring God's justice against unrepentant evil empires.

Theologically, Nineveh exemplifies God's sovereignty over nations, the power of His word to bring repentance (even among Israel's foes), and the tension between mercy and judgment—repent now, or face wrath. As Jesus references it, Nineveh's response indicts those who reject greater revelation (Matthew 12:41). It reminds us that no city, nation, or heart is beyond God's reach if they humble themselves before Him.
What is Epicureanism? What did Epicurus teach?

Epicureanism is an ancient Greek philosophical school founded by Epicurus (341–270 BC), who established a community called the Garden in Athens. It teaches that the highest good in life is pleasure, understood not as wild indulgence but as the absence of physical pain (aponia) and mental disturbance (ataraxia), achieved through simple living, moderation, and rational thought.

Epicurus's key teachings include:

- **Materialism and atomism**: The universe consists solely of atoms moving in a void, with no purposeful design or divine intervention. Everything happens by chance.
- **Gods and religion**: Gods exist as perfect, blissful beings in distant spaces between worlds, but they neither reward nor punish humans and take no interest in human affairs. Fear of gods or afterlife judgment is irrational and a source of unnecessary anxiety.
- **Death and the soul**: Death is the end of sensation—"When we exist, death is not; when death exists, we are not"—so it should not be feared. The soul is material and dissolves with the body, meaning no afterlife torment.
- **Pleasure as the guide**: Pursue modest pleasures like friendship, intellectual discussion, and basic needs (food, shelter), while avoiding excess, politics, and fame, which lead to pain. Epicurus famously said, "Eat bread and water, and call yourself happy."
- **Ethics and friendship**: Virtue arises naturally from seeking pleasure wisely; true friendship is the greatest source of security and joy.

Epicureanism influenced later thinkers like Lucretius and modern ideas of hedonism, but it starkly contrasts with the Bible's view of a personal Creator God who judges justly and offers eternal life through Christ (Romans 1:20; John 5:28-29). If you're exploring this for deeper reasons, what draws you to it?
What is the United Pentecostal Church?

The United Pentecostal Church International (UPCI) is the largest Oneness Pentecostal denomination, formed in 1945 through a merger of earlier Pentecostal groups emphasizing a "Oneness" view of God. It has grown significantly, with churches worldwide, and stresses experiential faith, evangelism, holiness living (including modest dress and separation from worldly practices), and spiritual gifts like speaking in tongues.

Key distinctives include:
- **Oneness theology**: They teach that God is a single person who reveals Himself in different modes or manifestations—Father in creation, Son in redemption, and Holy Spirit in regeneration—rejecting the doctrine of the Trinity as three distinct persons in one Godhead. This view, known as modalism, differs from the historic Christian understanding found in passages like Matthew 28:19 (baptizing in the name of the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit) and 2 Corinthians 13:14 (the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ, the love of God, and the fellowship of the Holy Spirit).
- **Salvation requirements**: Beyond faith in Christ, they emphasize water baptism by immersion in "Jesus' name only" (not the Trinitarian formula), receiving the Holy Spirit with the initial evidence of speaking in tongues, and living a holy life. This contrasts with justification by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, as taught in Ephesians 2:8-9.
- **Worship style**: Vibrant services with music, prophecy, healing, and tongues, alongside strong missions focus.

While UPCI members sincerely love Jesus and seek to follow Him, their rejection of the Trinity places them outside the bounds of orthodox Christianity as defined by the early creeds and Scripture's clear teaching on God's triune nature (Deuteronomy 6:4; Matthew 3:16-17). Many evangelicals view this as a significant doctrinal divide, akin to other unitarian groups historically.

If you're exploring faith or have questions about biblical Christianity, I'd encourage prayerfully studying Scripture yourself—perhaps starting with the Gospel of John—and connecting with a church that affirms the essentials like the Trinity. What draws your interest to this group?
What is Deuteronomistic History?

The Deuteronomistic History is a scholarly term describing a unified theological narrative spanning several books of the Old Testament: Deuteronomy, Joshua, Judges, 1 Samuel, 2 Samuel, and 1 Kings through 2 Kings. Proposed by historian Martin Noth in the mid-20th century, it views these books as a cohesive work edited by one or more authors (or schools) during or after Israel's exile in Babylon (around the 6th century BC).

This history retells Israel's story from the renewal of the Mosaic covenant on the plains of Moab (Deuteronomy) through the conquest of Canaan, the judges period, the united monarchy under Saul, David, and Solomon, and the divided kingdoms of Israel and Judah until their falls to Assyria (722 BC) and Babylon (586 BC). Central to it is "Deuteronomic theology," which emphasizes covenant faithfulness to Yahweh: obedience brings blessing and prosperity (Deuteronomy 28:1-14), while idolatry and injustice lead to curse, defeat, and exile (Deuteronomy 28:15-68; Joshua 1:7-8; Judges 2:11-23; 2 Kings 17:7-23; 2 Kings 23:26-27).

From a biblical perspective, this framework highlights God's sovereignty, the seriousness of sin, and the need for heartfelt obedience to His law, pointing forward to the ultimate fulfillment in Christ who perfectly keeps the covenant and redeems His people (Hebrews 8:6-13). It's a helpful lens for tracing God's faithful dealings with Israel despite their repeated unfaithfulness, underscoring themes echoed throughout Scripture.
Book of Philippians - Bible Survey

### Overview of Philippians

The Book of Philippians is a joyful letter from the Apostle Paul, written to the church he founded in Philippi during his second missionary journey (Acts 16:11-40). Paul, along with Timothy, authors it from prison—likely in Rome around AD 60-62 (Philippians 1:13-14)—yet radiates unshakeable joy amid suffering. Philippi, a Roman colony in Macedonia, was the first European church planted by Paul, supported by faithful partners like Lydia, the jailer, and later Euodia and Syntyche (Philippians 4:2).

#### Purpose
Paul writes to thank the Philippians for their generous support (Philippians 4:10-18), encourage unity amid conflict (Philippians 4:2-3), warn against false teachers (Philippians 3:2), and urge joyful perseverance in gospel partnership, regardless of circumstances (Philippians 1:3-5; 4:4).

#### Key Themes
- **Joy in Christ**: Joy appears over 16 times, rooted not in situations but in Christ's lordship, gospel advance, and fellowship (Philippians 1:18; 4:4). Paul rejoices even in chains, modeling that true joy transcends trials (Philippians 1:12-14).
- **Humility and Unity**: Emulate Christ's self-emptying mindset (Philippians 2:5-11), pursuing others' interests for church harmony (Philippians 2:1-4).
- **Gospel-Centered Living**: Press on toward Christlikeness, counting all else loss for knowing Him (Philippians 3:7-14); citizenship is in heaven (Philippians 3:20).
- **Contentment**: Learn secret of sufficiency in Christ through any circumstance (Philippians 4:11-13).

#### Outline
1. **Greeting and Thanksgiving (Philippians 1:1-11)**: Paul's prayer for their love to abound in knowledge and discernment.
2. **Gospel Advance in Suffering (Philippians 1:12-30)**: Chains proclaim Christ; live worthy as lights (Philippians 1:27-28).
3. **Christ's Example of Humility (Philippians 2:1-18)**: Famous "Christ hymn" (Philippians 2:5-11) on incarnation, obedience, exaltation; shine as stars.
4. **Faithful Co-Workers (Philippians 2:19-30)**: Praise for Timothy and Epaphroditus.
5. **Know Christ Above All (Philippians 3:1-11)**: Reject legalism; gain Christ by faith.
6. **Straining Forward (Philippians 3:12-21)**: Forget past, pursue the upward call; enemies of the cross contrasted.
7. **Final Exhortations (Philippians 4:1-9)**: Reconcile, rejoice, pray with thanksgiving; God's peace guards hearts.
8. **Gratitude for Partnership (Philippians 4:10-23)**: God supplies all needs; grace be with you.

#### Key Verses
- Philippians 1:21: "For to me to live is Christ, and to die is gain."
- Philippians 2:5: "Have this mind among yourselves, which is yours in Christ Jesus."
- Philippians 3:8: "I count everything as loss because of the surpassing worth of knowing Christ Jesus my Lord."
- Philippians 4:13: "I can do all things through him who strengthens me."

Philippians beautifully displays the Gospel's power: Christ's humility secures our joy, fueling unity and mission. As believers, it calls us to rejoice always (Philippians 4:4), fix eyes on Jesus (Philippians 3:14; Hebrews 12:2), and advance the Gospel together. I encourage you to read it slowly this week—perhaps aloud—and pray for its truths to deepen your walk with Christ. What stands out to you, or how might God use it in your life right now?
In what ways was Moses like Jesus?

The Bible draws striking parallels between Moses and Jesus Christ, portraying Moses as a God-appointed "type" or foreshadowing of the greater deliverer to come. Here are key ways they align, grounded in Scripture:

- **Miraculous deliverers from bondage**: Moses led Israel out of slavery in Egypt through God's power (Exodus 12:31-42; 14:21-31), just as Jesus delivers sinners from slavery to sin and death (John 8:34-36; Romans 6:17-18).
- **Prophets like unto one another**: God promised through Moses, "The Lord your God will raise up for you a prophet like me from among you" (Deuteronomy 18:15-18), fulfilled in Jesus, the ultimate Prophet who speaks God's words perfectly (Acts 3:22-23; John 6:14; Hebrews 1:1-2).
- **Faithful mediators and intercessors**: Moses stood between a holy God and sinful Israel, interceding during rebellion (Exodus 32:11-14, 30-32), prefiguring Christ's superior mediation as our High Priest (Hebrews 3:1-6; 1 Timothy 2:5; Hebrews 7:25).
- **Miracle-workers confirming God's message**: Moses performed signs like turning water to blood and parting the sea (Exodus 7:20; 14:21), while Jesus did greater works like calming storms and raising the dead (Mark 4:39; John 11:43-44), both authenticating their divine commission (John 5:36).
- **Lawgivers on mountains**: Moses received the Law on Sinai amid thunder and fire (Exodus 19:16-20; 20:1-17), and Jesus fulfilled it with authoritative teaching on a mountain (Matthew 5-7).
- **Rejected by their own people initially**: Moses was rejected by Israel (Exodus 2:11-15; Acts 7:25-35), as Jesus was by many Jews (John 1:11; Luke 4:16-30).
- **Lifegiving exaltation**: Moses lifted up a bronze serpent for healing (Numbers 21:8-9), symbolizing Jesus lifted on the cross for eternal life (John 3:14-15).
- **Extended fasts in preparation**: Both fasted forty days and nights before key ministries (Exodus 34:28; Deuteronomy 9:9; Matthew 4:1-2).

Hebrews 3:1-6 clarifies the distinction: "Moses was faithful in all God's house as a servant, to testify to the things that were to be spoken later, but Christ is faithful over God's house as a son." Moses points us to Jesus, the superior Prophet, Priest, and King. If you're exploring these truths, consider reading Hebrews 3 alongside Exodus—may it draw you closer to Christ, who offers forgiveness through His perfect life, death, and resurrection (Hebrews 12:2). What aspect of this comparison stands out to you?
What is Socinianism?

Socinianism is a rationalistic, anti-Trinitarian theological movement that emerged in the 16th century, primarily through the teachings of Italian reformers Laelius Socinus (1525–1562) and his nephew Faustus Socinus (1539–1604). It gained influence among the Polish Brethren (a radical Anabaptist group) before spreading to other parts of Europe.

### Key Beliefs of Socinianism
- **Rejection of the Trinity**: Socinians denied the doctrine of one God in three persons (Father, Son, and Holy Spirit), viewing God as a single person and the Holy Spirit as merely God's power or influence, not a divine person. This contradicts passages like Matthew 28:19, where Jesus commands baptism "in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit," and 2 Corinthians 13:14, which offers the grace of Christ, love of God, and fellowship of the Holy Spirit.
- **Denial of Christ's Deity**: They taught that Jesus was a mere human prophet or teacher, exalted by God for his moral example but not eternally God incarnate. This opposes clear biblical affirmations of Christ's divinity, such as John 1:1 ("In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God") and John 1:14 (the Word became flesh).
- **Rejection of Penal Substitutionary Atonement**: Socinians viewed Christ's death not as a sacrificial payment for sin but as a moral influence or example to inspire obedience. They denied original sin's guilt and the need for a divine substitute, undermining texts like Isaiah 53:5-6 (He was pierced for our transgressions) and Romans 5:8 (Christ died for us while we were sinners).
- **Salvation by Works and Reason**: Salvation comes through rational moral improvement and imitating Christ, rather than grace alone through faith alone in Christ's finished work. This stands against Ephesians 2:8-9 ("For by grace you have been saved through faith... not a result of works").

Socinianism influenced later Unitarianism and modern liberal theologies but was rejected by orthodox Christianity as it undermines the gospel's core: God's triune nature, Christ's deity and atoning death, and justification by faith (Galatians 2:16). Historic creeds like the Nicene Creed (325 AD) and Athanasian Creed affirm the biblical truths it denies.

If you'd like more on how this relates to Scripture or church history, feel free to ask!
Can an unmarried man be a deacon or elder?

Yes, an unmarried man can qualify to serve as a deacon or elder, provided he meets all the other biblical qualifications for the role.

The key phrase in Scripture is "the husband of one wife" (Greek: *mias gynaikos andra*, literally "a one-woman man"), which appears for overseers/elders in 1 Timothy 3:2 and Titus 1:6, and for deacons in 1 Timothy 3:12. This emphasizes character—fidelity, faithfulness, and moral purity in relationships—rather than mandating marriage. It prohibits polygamy, promiscuity, or divorce/remarriage in ways that disqualify (while allowing biblical exceptions like widowhood), but it does not require a man to be married. An unmarried man can fully embody being a "one-woman man" by demonstrating self-control, sobriety, and respectability in singleness (1 Timothy 3:2; Titus 1:8).

Other qualifications apply equally, such as being above reproach, temperate, prudent, respectable, hospitable, able to teach (for elders), not addicted to wine, not greedy, managing his household well (if applicable), and having a good reputation (1 Timothy 3:1-7, 8-13; Titus 1:5-9). Singleness can even be an asset, freeing a man for undivided devotion to ministry (1 Corinthians 7:32-35).

Many faithful churches across traditions ordain single men to these offices when they prove godly over time. If you're discerning this calling or serving alongside someone, pray for wisdom and seek counsel from mature elders (James 3:1; Acts 20:28). What specific situation prompts your question?
Is it better, spiritually, to be poor than to be rich?

No, it is not inherently better spiritually to be poor than to be rich. What matters most to God is the posture of your heart toward money and possessions, regardless of your economic status (1 Timothy 6:6-10; Matthew 6:19-21).

Scripture warns of the spiritual dangers that often accompany wealth—such as self-reliance, idolatry, or neglect of the poor (Mark 10:23-25; Luke 12:15-21; James 5:1-5)—but it also celebrates faithful rich believers like Abraham, Job, and Zacchaeus, who used their resources generously for God's kingdom (Genesis 13:2; Job 1:3; Luke 19:8). Conversely, poverty does not guarantee spiritual vitality; the poor can harbor resentment, envy, or discontent, while the rich can cultivate humility, generosity, and dependence on God (Proverbs 30:7-9; Philippians 4:11-13).

Jesus calls all of us—rich or poor—to store up treasures in heaven by loving God supremely, seeking His kingdom first, and stewarding whatever we have with contentment and generosity (Matthew 6:33; Hebrews 13:5; 2 Corinthians 9:6-7). True spiritual richness flows from treasuring Christ above all earthly gain. If wealth tempts you toward pride or hoarding, pray for a generous heart; if poverty weighs heavy, trust God's provision as your shepherd (Psalm 23:1). In either case, godliness with contentment is great gain.
Will the sun really be turned to darkness and the moon to blood (Joel 2:31)?

Yes, Joel 2:31 describes a striking prophetic sign: "The sun shall be turned to darkness, and the moon to blood, before the great and awesome day of the Lord comes." The prophet Joel uses this imagery as part of his vision of the "day of the Lord," a recurring biblical theme pointing to God's decisive judgment and intervention in history (Joel 2:1-11; Amos 5:18-20; Zephaniah 1:14-18).

This verse finds initial fulfillment in the outpouring of the Holy Spirit at Pentecost, as Peter quotes it directly in Acts 2:20 to explain the events of that day. Yet the full realization awaits the future, echoed in Jesus' words about cosmic disturbances before His return (Matthew 24:29) and John's vision in Revelation 6:12 of the sun becoming black and the moon like blood amid God's judgments.

Faithful Christians interpret these signs in varied ways, as eschatology involves less explicit details in Scripture, allowing for different emphases:
- **Literal view**: Many premillennial believers see future astronomical events as actual harbingers of Christ's second coming, signaling the end of the age.
- **Symbolic view**: Amillennial and postmillennial interpreters often view them as apocalyptic language depicting spiritual darkness, upheaval, and divine wrath, much like Old Testament prophecies (Isaiah 13:10; Ezekiel 32:7).

These differences stem from how we read prophetic genres—poetic/hyperbolic or straightforward—and do not divide core Christian unity, as all agree Christ will return bodily to judge and renew creation (Acts 1:11; 2 Peter 3:10-13). Scripture urges readiness not through decoding signs but trusting Christ: "Watch therefore, for you know neither the day nor the hour" (Matthew 25:13).

Study Joel alongside the New Testament, pray for wisdom (James 1:5), and rest in the certainty of Jesus' victory. What draws you to this passage?
Who were the kings of Israel and Judah?

The united kingdom of Israel under Saul, David, and Solomon divided around 931 BC after Solomon's death (1 Kings 11:43; 12:1-20), splitting into the northern kingdom of Israel (10 tribes, capital Samaria) and the southern kingdom of Judah (2 tribes, capital Jerusalem). The Bible records their kings primarily in 1 Kings 12–2 Kings 25 and 2 Chronicles 10–36. All northern kings were wicked, leading to Israel's fall to Assyria in 722 BC (2 Kings 17:1-23). Judah's kings varied, with the Davidic line preserved until the Babylonian exile in 586 BC (2 Kings 25:1-21), fulfilling God's covenant promises (2 Samuel 7:12-16).

### Kings of Judah (Southern Kingdom, all from David's line except Athaliah)
Presented chronologically with approximate reign years BC (accounting for co-regencies):

- Rehoboam (931–913; 1 Kings 14:21)
- Abijah (also Abijam; 913–911; 1 Kings 15:1)
- Asa (911–870; 1 Kings 15:9)
- Jehoshaphat (870–848; 1 Kings 22:41)
- Jehoram (also Joram; 848–841; 2 Kings 8:16)
- Ahaziah (841; 2 Kings 8:25)
- Athaliah (queen, usurper; 841–835; 2 Kings 11:1; 2 Chronicles 22:10)
- Joash (also Jehoash; 835–796; 2 Kings 12:1)
- Amaziah (796–767; 2 Kings 14:1)
- Uzziah (also Azariah; 767–740; 2 Kings 15:1)
- Jotham (740–732; 2 Kings 15:32)
- Ahaz (732–716; 2 Kings 16:1)
- Hezekiah (716–687; 2 Kings 18:1)
- Manasseh (687–642; 2 Kings 21:1)
- Amon (642–640; 2 Kings 21:19)
- Josiah (640–609; 2 Kings 22:1)
- Jehoahaz (609; 2 Kings 23:31)
- Jehoiakim (609–598; 2 Kings 23:34)
- Jehoiachin (also Jeconiah/Coniah; 598; 2 Kings 24:8)
- Zedekiah (597–586; 2 Kings 24:17)

### Kings of Israel (Northern Kingdom)
All considered evil in Scripture (1 Kings 16:25–33; 2 Kings 3:1–17:2):

- Jeroboam I (931–910; 1 Kings 12:20)
- Nadab (910–909; 1 Kings 15:25)
- Baasha (909–886; 1 Kings 15:27)
- Elah (886–885; 1 Kings 16:8)
- Zimri (885, 7 days; 1 Kings 16:15)
- Omri (885–874; 1 Kings 16:23)
- Ahab (874–853; 1 Kings 16:29)
- Ahaziah (853–852; 1 Kings 22:51)
- Joram (also Jehoram; 852–841; 2 Kings 3:1)
- Jehu (841–814; 2 Kings 9:1–10:36)
- Jehoahaz (814–798; 2 Kings 13:1)
- Joash (also Jehoash; 798–782; 2 Kings 13:10)
- Jeroboam II (793–753, co-regent earlier; 2 Kings 14:23)
- Zechariah (753–752; 2 Kings 15:8)
- Shallum (752, 1 month; 2 Kings 15:13)
- Menahem (752–742; 2 Kings 15:17)
- Pekahiah (742–740; 2 Kings 15:23)
- Pekah (752–732, co-regent; 2 Kings 15:27)
- Hoshea (732–722; 2 Kings 17:1)

These reigns reflect God's sovereign history, warning against idolatry while preserving hope in Messiah from Judah's line (Genesis 49:10; Isaiah 9:6-7). For deeper study, read the books of Kings alongside Chronicles.
What is Euthyphro’s Dilemma?

Euthyphro’s Dilemma comes from Plato’s dialogue *Euthyphro*, where Socrates asks: Is something pious (or morally good) because the gods love it, or do the gods love it because it is pious? In modern terms, applied to Christian theology, it poses the question: Is something good because God commands it, or does God command it because it is independently good?

This creates a false dichotomy for Christians. God’s commands are not arbitrary, nor does He conform to an external standard of goodness. Instead, moral goodness is grounded in God’s unchanging, holy nature—He *is* good, and His commands reflect His perfect character (Psalm 119:68; James 1:17). As the sovereign Creator, God defines goodness without caprice, ensuring morality is objective and reliable (Malachi 3:6).

This dilemma invites us to trust God’s revelation in Scripture as the ultimate guide for discerning good, fostering worship of Him as the source of all true goodness.
Who was Og king of Bashan?

Og, king of Bashan, was a giant ruler of a fortified Amorite kingdom east of the Jordan River, in the region known as Bashan (Deuteronomy 3:1-13; Numbers 21:33-35). He is described as the last of the Rephaim, a race of giants, whose enormous iron bedstead—nine cubits long and four cubits wide (about 13.5 by 6 feet)—was displayed in Rabbah as evidence of his stature (Deuteronomy 3:11).

During Israel's wilderness journey under Moses, Og marched out with his army to confront them at Edrei, but God delivered him into Israel's hands, and they utterly destroyed him, his sons, and his people, without survivors (Numbers 21:33-35; Deuteronomy 3:3-7). This victory cleared the way for the tribes of Reuben, Gad, and half of Manasseh to settle the land (Deuteronomy 3:13; Joshua 13:30-31). Og's defeat is later listed among the conquered kings in Joshua 12:4-5 and 13:12, underscoring God's faithfulness in fulfilling His promises to Israel.

This account reminds us of God's sovereign power over even the mightiest earthly rulers, as Psalm 135:10-11 celebrates: "He struck down many nations and killed mighty kings—Sihon king of the Amorites, Og king of Bashan, and all the kingdoms of Canaan."
Who were the Hellenistic Jews in the Bible?

Hellenistic Jews, also called Hellenists (from the Greek *Hellēnistai*), were Jews of the Diaspora who primarily spoke Koine Greek, were culturally influenced by Hellenistic (Greek) society, and often lived in Gentile-majority cities like Alexandria, Antioch, and Ephesus. They read the Greek translation of the Old Testament (the Septuagint) and were distinct from the "Hebrews," who spoke Aramaic or Hebrew and were more tied to Palestinian Jewish traditions.

The New Testament mentions them explicitly in the early church context:
- In Acts 6:1, a dispute arose when "the Hellenistic Jews among them complained against the Hebraic Jews because their widows were being overlooked in the daily distribution of food." This led to the appointment of the first seven deacons, including Stephen and Philip, several of whom had Greek names.
- In Acts 9:29, Saul (later Paul), after his conversion, "spoke and debated with the Hellenistic Jews, but they tried to kill him."

These references highlight tensions and unity efforts in the Jerusalem church between cultural groups, showing God's work among diverse believers as the Gospel spread (Ephesians 2:14-16).
How can I overcome spiritual apathy?

Spiritual apathy is a common struggle for believers, like the church in Ephesus who had forsaken their first love (Revelation 2:4). Yet God calls us back with tenderness: "Remember therefore from where you have fallen; repent, and do the works you did at first" (Revelation 2:5). Take heart—He is faithful to rekindle what has grown cold, as He works in you both to will and to work for His good pleasure (Philippians 2:13).

Here are biblically grounded steps to stir your heart anew, relying on the Holy Spirit through the ordinary means of grace:

1. **Confess and repent honestly.** Acknowledge your apathy to God in prayer, asking Him to search your heart (Psalm 139:23-24). Repentance revives joy, as David prayed after his wandering: "Restore to me the joy of your salvation" (Psalm 51:12).

2. **Feast on Scripture daily.** Apathy often stems from neglecting God's Word, which is living and active (Hebrews 4:12). Start small—read a Psalm and a Gospel chapter each morning, meditating on Christ's beauty and sacrifice. Let truths like Psalm 42:1-2 reshape your longings: "As a deer pants for flowing streams, so pants my soul for you, O God."

3. **Pray with dependence.** Pour out your dryness to the Lord, perhaps using the Lord's Prayer (Matthew 6:9-13) or cries like "Out of the depths I cry to you, O Lord!" (Psalm 130:1). Pray with a trusted brother or sister in Christ for mutual encouragement (Hebrews 3:13; James 5:16).

4. **Pursue fellowship and obedience.** Isolation fuels apathy—gather with your local church for worship, the Lord's Supper, and exhortation (Hebrews 10:24-25). Obey in small things, like serving others, as fervor comes from not being slothful in zeal (Romans 12:11).

5. **Rest in Christ's finished work.** Don't strive in your own strength; the same God who saved you completes the good work He began (Philippians 1:6). Fix your eyes on Jesus, who endured the cross for joy set before Him (Hebrews 12:2).

Persist in these, even when feelings lag—faithfulness precedes fervor. What specific area of your walk feels most apathetic right now, like prayer or serving? I'd love to pray alongside you in this by pointing you to Scripture.
How can I experience healing and recovery after an abortion?

I'm so sorry for the deep pain, grief, and regret you're carrying after an abortion. God sees your heart, knows every tear, and offers profound healing through His mercy and grace. Psalm 34:18 assures us, "The Lord is near to the brokenhearted and saves the crushed in spirit."

First, know this: No sin is beyond God's forgiveness. He is holy and just, and our choices like abortion separate us from Him due to sin (Isaiah 59:2), but Jesus Christ—fully God and fully man—lived perfectly, died on the cross in our place to bear the penalty for all sin (including this one), was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, conquering death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). Salvation, including forgiveness and new life, comes by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone—not by our works or goodness (Ephesians 2:8-9). You can turn to Him now: repent of sin, trust in Jesus' finished work on the cross for you, and receive His full pardon (1 John 1:9; Romans 10:9).

To experience healing:
- **Confess and receive forgiveness directly from God.** Pour out your heart to Him in prayer—He invites it (Psalm 62:8). Name the loss, the guilt, the "what ifs," and claim His promise: "If we confess our sins, he is faithful and just to forgive us our sins and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness" (1 John 1:9).
- **Immerse in Scripture.** Meditate on passages like Psalm 103:11-12 ("For as high as the heavens are above the earth, so great is his steadfast love toward those who fear him; as far as the east is from the west, so far does he remove our transgressions from us"); Lamentations 3:22-23 (His mercies are new every morning); and 2 Corinthians 5:17 (in Christ, you are a new creation).
- **Seek godly community.** Connect with a local Bible-believing church where elders or mature believers can walk with you—James 5:16 says, "Therefore, confess your sins to one another and pray for one another, that you may be healed. The prayer of a righteous person has great power as it is working." Consider a certified biblical counselor through ACBC (Association of Certified Biblical Counselors) for structured support.
- **Grieve healthily.** Journal, lament in prayer (as David did in Psalms), or join a post-abortion recovery group like those from ministries such as Rachel's Vineyard or your church, always rooted in the Gospel.
- **Rest in His care.** Philippians 1:6 promises, "He who began a good work in you will bring it to completion." Healing is progressive, by the Holy Spirit through Word, prayer, and fellowship.

This isn't about earning forgiveness—it's receiving what Christ has already accomplished. What part of this resonates most, or how can I pray with you in mind as you turn to Him? God is making all things new (Revelation 21:5).
What are the Major Prophets and Minor Prophets?

The Old Testament prophetic books are traditionally divided into the Major Prophets and Minor Prophets, a classification based on the length of the books rather than their importance—all are equally inspired by God as part of His authoritative Word (2 Timothy 3:16-17; 2 Peter 1:20-21).

### Major Prophets
These are the longer prophetic books, placed first in the English Old Testament canon:
- **Isaiah** (66 chapters): Prophecies of judgment, Messiah's coming, and restoration.
- **Jeremiah** (52 chapters): Warnings to Judah and promises of a new covenant.
- **Lamentations** (5 chapters): Poetic laments over Jerusalem's fall (attributed to Jeremiah).
- **Ezekiel** (48 chapters): Visions of God's glory, judgment, and future temple.
- **Daniel** (12 chapters): Stories of faithfulness in exile and apocalyptic visions.

### Minor Prophets
These twelve shorter books, often called "The Twelve" in the Hebrew Bible (forming one scroll), follow the Major Prophets:
- **Hosea**: God's faithful love amid Israel's unfaithfulness.
- **Joel**: Locust plague as judgment and promise of the Spirit.
- **Amos**: Justice and righteousness for Israel and nations.
- **Obadiah**: Judgment on Edom.
- **Jonah**: God's mercy to Nineveh.
- **Micah**: Justice, mercy, and Bethlehem's ruler.
- **Nahum**: Nineveh's downfall.
- **Habakkuk**: Wrestling with God's justice amid evil.
- **Zephaniah**: Day of the Lord and restoration.
- **Haggai**: Rebuilding the temple.
- **Zechariah**: Visions of restoration and Messiah.
- **Malachi**: Covenant faithfulness and coming messenger.

This division helps organize the prophets' messages of God's holiness, human rebellion, judgment, and ultimate redemption through the coming Messiah, pointing forward to Jesus Christ (Luke 24:25-27; Acts 10:43). If you'd like to study a specific prophet, let me know!
What does God mean when He says, “Seek me and live” (Amos 5:4)?

In Amos 5:4, God speaks through the prophet to wayward Israel amid warnings of coming judgment for their idolatry, injustice, and empty rituals: "For thus says the Lord to the house of Israel: 'Seek me and live.'" This is a urgent, merciful call to turn from false gods and superficial religion back to the Lord Himself.

"Seek me" means wholeheartedly pursuing God in repentance, obedience, and true worship—not mere outward forms like festivals or sacrifices without a changed heart (Amos 5:21-24). It's echoed throughout Scripture: "You will seek me and find me, when you seek me with all your heart" (Jeremiah 29:13); "But from there you will seek the Lord your God and you will find him, if you search after him with all your heart and with all your soul" (Deuteronomy 4:29).

"And live" contrasts deathly judgment with the life God offers—preservation from destruction in their immediate context, but pointing to abundant, eternal life for those who truly turn to Him (Deuteronomy 30:19-20; Psalm 69:32).

This promise finds its ultimate fulfillment in Jesus Christ, who invites us, "Seek and you will find" (Matthew 7:7), and declares, "I am the way, and the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me" (John 14:6). To seek God today means repenting of sin, trusting in Christ's atoning death and resurrection for forgiveness, and following Him daily through His Word, prayer, and obedience (Hebrews 11:6).

Friend, if your heart is stirred, seek Him now—turn from anything that rivals Him and trust Jesus alone for life everlasting. What is the Lord prompting in you through this verse?
If aborted babies go to heaven, why is abortion wrong?

Abortion is profoundly wrong because it deliberately ends the life of an innocent human being created in God's image, violating His clear command against murder (Exodus 20:13; Genesis 1:27; Genesis 9:6). Scripture affirms that God forms and knows each person intimately from the moment of conception—"For you formed my inward parts; you knitted me together in my mother's womb" (Psalm 139:13)—and sets life apart as sacred, appointing its beginning and end (Job 14:5; Deuteronomy 32:39).

Even if God, in His mercy, receives the souls of unborn children who die (as many Christians infer from passages like 2 Samuel 12:23 and Jesus' welcome of children in Matthew 19:14), this does not make the act right. Sin remains sin against a holy God, regardless of the victim's eternal destiny or the perpetrator's potential forgiveness. It's like asking why theft is wrong if the stolen goods are returned and the thief forgiven—the act usurps God's sovereign authority, grieves His heart (Genesis 6:6), wounds the mother and family, and devalues all human life in society.

The good news is that no sin, including abortion, is beyond Christ's atoning blood. If you've been involved or are struggling, turn to Him in repentance and faith: "If we confess our sins, he is faithful and just to forgive us our sins and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness" (1 John 1:9). Find healing through a local church, biblical counseling, or ministries like Rachel's Vineyard. God redeems and restores for His glory. What aspect of this weighs on your heart?
Should communion be open or closed?

The question of open or closed communion (also called the Lord's Supper) is a matter of Christian liberty where faithful believers disagree, rooted in how churches interpret passages like 1 Corinthians 11:17-34, which calls for self-examination and worthy participation to avoid judgment, and 1 Corinthians 10:16-17, which emphasizes the unity of Christ's body.

Churches practicing **closed communion** typically restrict it to baptized members of their own congregation or denomination, often to uphold church discipline (as in 1 Corinthians 5:11, excluding the unrepentant) and ensure participants understand the ordinance's meaning. This reflects local church autonomy and elder oversight.

Churches practicing **open communion** welcome all professing believers from other congregations, prioritizing the gospel's unifying power across the body of Christ (as seen in Acts 2:46, where early believers broke bread together) while still urging personal discernment.

Scripture does not explicitly mandate one practice, leading to these differences in application. As with other non-essentials, Romans 14:1-5 encourages accepting one another without quarrels, pursuing peace and mutual edification. I affirm the spiritual presence of Christ in the Supper for believers, but the precise table policies belong to each local church's elders under Scripture's guidance.

Discuss this with your church leaders—they know your context best—and focus together on proclaiming Christ's death until He returns (1 Corinthians 11:26). What draws you to this question?
What is the Remnant Fellowship?

Remnant Fellowship is a religious organization founded in 1999 by Gwen Shamblin Lara (who passed away in a plane crash in 2021) in Brentwood, Tennessee. It originated from her Weigh Down Workshop, a program that teaches weight loss and healthy eating as acts of obedience to God, viewing overeating and gluttony as key sins to overcome through reliance on the Holy Spirit rather than dieting.

The group describes itself as a Christian church emphasizing total devotion to God, repentance from idolatry (including food, media, and materialism), and strong community accountability. It now operates under leadership from Shamblin's children, Michael Shamblin and Elizabeth Shamblin Hannah.

From a biblical perspective, Remnant Fellowship holds some views that diverge from historic Christian essentials. For instance, it rejects the doctrine of the Trinity, teaching that God is one person and that Jesus is the Son of God but not eternally co-equal with the Father—a position that conflicts with passages like Matthew 28:19 and 2 Corinthians 13:14, which affirm one God in three persons. It also places heavy emphasis on outward obedience and law-keeping as evidence of salvation, which can risk overshadowing justification by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8-9; Romans 3:28).

While they affirm Christ's deity and atonement in some ways, these departures from core doctrines warrant careful discernment, as Scripture urges believers to "test the spirits to see whether they are from God" (1 John 4:1). I encourage evaluating any group against the full counsel of God's Word, seeking counsel from trusted pastors rooted in orthodox Christianity. If you're exploring faith communities, a local Bible-believing church grounded in the Gospel is a great place to start. What draws your interest in this group?
Who wrote the book of 2 John? Who was the author of 2 John?

The Apostle John wrote the book of 2 John. The author identifies himself simply as "the elder" (2 John 1:1), a title fitting for John as a senior leader in the late first-century church. This short epistle shares striking similarities in vocabulary, themes (such as truth, love, obedience, and warnings against false teachers), and style with the Gospel of John, 1 John, and 3 John—all traditionally attributed to the same author. Early church fathers, including Irenaeus (around AD 180) and Tertullian, unanimously affirmed John's authorship, aligning with the consistent testimony of Scripture's inspiration (2 Timothy 3:16; 2 Peter 1:20-21).
What is false guilt, and how can I avoid it?

False guilt is the lingering sense of shame or condemnation that persists even after genuine confession and repentance, or it arises from things that aren't actual sins against God—like perfectionism, others' unrealistic expectations, or Satan's accusations as "the accuser of our brothers" (Revelation 12:10). It differs from true guilt, which is God's conviction over real sin (John 16:8), prompting us to confess and turn to Him.

To avoid it, anchor yourself in God's promises of forgiveness:

1. **Confess specifically and receive forgiveness fully.** When you sin, agree with God about it right away—"If we confess our sins, He is faithful and just to forgive us our sins and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness" (1 John 1:9). Don't replay it endlessly; trust His word over your feelings.

2. **Reject condemnation in Christ.** There is now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus (Romans 8:1). Remind yourself daily: Jesus bore all your guilt on the cross, and God remembers your sins no more (Hebrews 10:17).

3. **Renew your mind with truth.** Combat lies with Scripture—meditate on Psalm 103:12 ("as far as the east is from the west, so far does He remove our transgressions from us") or Psalm 32:1-2 (blessed is the one whose sin the Lord does not count against him). Replace "I must have failed too much" with "I am forgiven and loved."

4. **Test guilt against God's standards.** Ask: Is this from Scripture, or cultural pressure? True guilt aligns with God's law (Exodus 20); false guilt often stems from legalism or comparison (Galatians 6:4).

5. **Seek wise counsel and accountability.** Share with a mature believer or pastor to gain perspective, as iron sharpens iron (Proverbs 27:17). Avoid isolation, where guilt festers.

As you rest in Christ's finished work, false guilt loses its power—He sets you free indeed (John 8:36). If this weighs heavy, pray honestly to God now, claiming His forgiveness, and consider reading Romans 8 slowly today for fresh assurance. How has guilt shown up for you lately?
Are baptism and communion means of grace?

Yes, baptism and the Lord's Supper (communion) are means of grace in the ordinary way God uses them to strengthen believers' faith, confirm covenant promises, and point us to Christ. They are not magical rituals that automatically confer saving grace apart from faith—salvation remains by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8-9)—but they are divinely appointed signs and seals that visibly represent and nourish spiritual realities already received by faith.

### Baptism as a Means of Grace
Baptism by immersion for believers is an outward sign of inward grace, symbolizing our union with Christ in His death, burial, and resurrection (Romans 6:3-4). Jesus commanded it as part of making disciples: "Go therefore and make disciples of all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit" (Matthew 28:19). Peter links it to repentance and the forgiveness proclaimed in Christ: "Repent and be baptized every one of you in the name of Jesus Christ for the forgiveness of your sins" (Acts 2:38). Through this ordinance, God ordinarily confirms His promises to those who trust in Christ, much like a visible sermon that stirs faith and obedience.

### The Lord's Supper as a Means of Grace
Communion proclaims Christ's death until He returns, with bread representing His body broken for us and the cup His blood of the new covenant (1 Corinthians 11:23-26; Matthew 26:26-28). It involves a spiritual presence of Christ by the Holy Spirit, nourishing our souls as we feed on Him by faith. Paul warns of unworthy participation, showing its power to convict, judge, or bless: "Whoever, therefore, eats the bread or drinks the cup of the Lord in an unworthy manner will be guilty concerning the body and blood of the Lord" (1 Corinthians 11:27). It's a family meal for the church that deepens fellowship with Christ and one another (1 Corinthians 10:16-17).

Christians across traditions (Baptists, Presbyterians, etc.) affirm these as means of grace alongside the Word, prayer, and fellowship, though we differ on details like infant baptism. If you're exploring this in your church life, I'd encourage discussing it with your pastor—participating worthily brings great encouragement from the Lord! What draws you to this question?
Who was Onesiphorus in the Bible?

Onesiphorus was a faithful Christian brother commended by the Apostle Paul for his kindness and courage during Paul's imprisonment in Rome. In 2 Timothy 1:16-18, Paul writes, "The Lord grant mercy to the household of Onesiphorus, for he often refreshed me and was not ashamed of my chains, but when he arrived in Rome he searched for me earnestly and found me—may the Lord grant him to find mercy from the Lord on that day!—and you well know all the service he rendered at Ephesus." Paul also sends greetings to "the household of Onesiphorus" in 2 Timothy 4:19, suggesting Onesiphorus may have passed away by then, as Paul addresses his family directly.

Onesiphorus stands as an inspiring example of selfless service, loyalty to the gospel amid persecution, and the blessing of "refreshing" weary servants of Christ, much like Philemon 20 encourages believers to bring joy and encouragement to one another.
What is the spirit of the law?

The "spirit of the law" refers to the true intent, purpose, and heart of God's commandments, which is to foster love for God and love for others, empowered by the Holy Spirit, rather than mere external or legalistic obedience to its written rules (the "letter of the law").

The Apostle Paul contrasts these in 2 Corinthians 3:6, where he says God "has made us sufficient to be ministers of a new covenant, not of the letter but of the Spirit. For the letter kills, but the Spirit gives life." The letter alone brings condemnation because no one can perfectly keep every rule, but the Spirit transforms the heart, enabling genuine obedience from within (Romans 2:29; Romans 7:6).

Jesus Himself summarized the entire Law and Prophets this way: "You shall love the Lord your God with all your heart and with all your soul and with all your mind. This is the great and first commandment. And a second is like it: You shall love your neighbor as yourself. On these two commandments depend all the Law and the Prophets" (Matthew 22:37-40). Paul echoes this: "Love does no wrong to a neighbor; therefore love is the fulfilling of the law" (Romans 13:10).

Living by the spirit of the law means walking in the Holy Spirit's fruit—love, joy, peace, patience, kindness, goodness, faithfulness, gentleness, and self-control (Galatians 5:22-23)—so our actions flow from transformed hearts in Christ, bringing true freedom and life. If you're seeking to apply this in a specific area of life, what situation comes to mind?
What are the seven deadly sins?

The seven deadly sins, also known as the capital vices or cardinal sins, are a traditional classification of particularly destructive human vices that lead to other immoral behaviors. This list originated in early Christian monastic writings (from figures like Evagrius Ponticus in the 4th century) and was popularized in the Middle Ages by Pope Gregory I and later in works like Dante's *Divine Comedy*. Importantly, the Bible does not explicitly list them as a set of seven, but it warns against each as forms of rebellion against God, stemming from the sinful heart (Mark 7:21-23).

Here they are, with brief descriptions and key biblical warnings:

1. **Pride** (superbia): Excessive self-focus or arrogance, seeing oneself above others or God. "Pride goes before destruction, and a haughty spirit before a fall" (Proverbs 16:18).
2. **Greed** (avaritia): Insatiable desire for wealth or possessions. "For the love of money is a root of all kinds of evils" (1 Timothy 6:10).
3. **Lust** (luxuria): Uncontrolled sexual desire or objectification. "But I say to you that everyone who looks at a woman with lustful intent has already committed adultery with her in his heart" (Matthew 5:28).
4. **Envy** (invidia): Resentment toward others' success or blessings. "For where jealousy and selfish ambition exist, there will be disorder and every vile practice" (James 3:16).
5. **Gluttony** (gula): Overindulgence in food, drink, or comfort. "Be not among drunkards or among gluttonous eaters of meat, for the drunkard and the glutton will come to poverty" (Proverbs 23:20-21).
6. **Wrath** (ira): Uncontrolled anger or hatred. "Be angry and do not sin; do not let the sun go down on your anger" (Ephesians 4:26).
7. **Sloth** (acedia): Spiritual or physical laziness, neglecting God-given duties. "The soul of the sluggard craves and gets nothing, while the soul of the diligent is richly supplied" (Proverbs 13:4).

These sins highlight how our hearts are inclined toward selfishness apart from God's grace (Romans 3:23). The good news is that through Christ's death and resurrection, forgiveness and transformation are available to all who repent and trust in Him (1 John 1:9; 2 Corinthians 5:17). If any of these resonate in your life, prayerfully examine your heart and seek God's help through Scripture and fellowship with believers. What prompted your question about them?
Should pastors be paid a salary?

Yes, pastors should be paid a salary, as Scripture clearly teaches that those who devote themselves to full-time gospel ministry deserve financial support from the church they serve.

The apostle Paul addresses this directly in 1 Timothy 5:17-18, where he writes, "Let the elders who rule well be considered worthy of double honor, especially those who labor in preaching and teaching. For the Scripture says, 'You shall not muzzle an ox when it treads out the grain,' and, 'The laborer deserves his wages.'" Here, Paul links "double honor" to tangible provision, drawing from Old Testament principles like Deuteronomy 25:4 and Numbers 18:21-24, where priests and Levites were sustained by the people's tithes since they had no inheritance of land.

Jesus Himself affirmed this in Luke 10:7, instructing His disciples, "for the laborer deserves his wages," and Paul expands on it in 1 Corinthians 9:7-14, arguing that just as soldiers are paid, farmers eat their crops, and priests live from temple offerings, so spiritual leaders have the right to be supported by the church without being burdened by secular work that hinders ministry.

That said, there is biblical liberty for bi-vocational pastors, as Paul himself tentmade at times (Acts 18:3) to avoid burdening churches or as a model of self-support (1 Corinthians 9:12). Local churches should prayerfully determine fair compensation based on need, church size, cost of living, and the pastor's family responsibilities (1 Timothy 5:8), ensuring it reflects generosity without excess (1 Timothy 3:2-3). If you're navigating this in your church, discuss it openly with elders, leaning on passages like 2 Corinthians 9:6-7 for cheerful, proportional giving.
What does it mean that man shall not live by bread alone (Deuteronomy 8:3)?

Man shall not live by bread alone means that physical food and material provision are not enough to sustain true life—humans ultimately depend on every word from God's mouth for survival, guidance, and flourishing. In Deuteronomy 8:3, God reminds Israel of their wilderness journey: He humbled them with hunger, then provided unfamiliar manna, not merely to feed their bodies but to teach that obedience to His commands sustains them more than daily bread. This verse underscores total reliance on God's faithful provision and instruction, as He alone gives life.

Jesus echoes this truth perfectly during His temptation in the wilderness (Matthew 4:4), refusing Satan's urging to turn stones into bread by declaring dependence on God's word over fleeting physical needs. For us today, it calls believers to prioritize Scripture as our daily spiritual nourishment—reading, meditating, and obeying it brings life to our souls amid trials, just as Jesus prayed in John 4:34 that doing the Father's will was His food. What is the significance of Lo Debar in the Bible?

Lo Debar was a town located east of the Jordan River, likely in the region of Gilead, mentioned primarily in the account of King David's kindness to Mephibosheth, the grandson of Saul and son of Jonathan (2 Samuel 9:1-13). The name "Lo Debar" in Hebrew means "no pasture" or "without pasture," evoking imagery of barrenness, desolation, and lack—fitting for the place where Mephibosheth, crippled from a childhood accident, lived in obscurity and poverty after the fall of Saul's house (2 Samuel 9:4).

Its significance unfolds in David's covenant faithfulness to his friendship with Jonathan (1 Samuel 20:14-17, 42). Despite Mephibosheth having no claim on David's mercy—he was lame, from an enemy royal line, and hidden away—David sought him out, restored Saul's lands to him, and invited him to eat at the king's table as one of his sons (2 Samuel 9:7, 11, 13). This act later recurs amid Absalom's rebellion, highlighting loyalty amid turmoil (2 Samuel 16:1-4; 19:24-30).

The story beautifully foreshadows God's grace in Christ: just as David pursued a helpless, undeserving man from a place of "no pasture" to royal fellowship, God seeks sinners from spiritual barrenness, offering restoration through Jesus' atoning work. Ephesians 2:4-7 describes how God, rich in mercy, makes us alive with Christ and seats us with Him in heavenly places, all by grace through faith. What a reminder of the Shepherd who leaves the ninety-nine to find the lost one (Luke 15:4-7)!
What does the Bible say about parenting?

The Bible presents parenting as a profound privilege and responsibility, reflecting God's fatherly care for us (Psalm 103:13). Children are a heritage from the Lord, like arrows in a warrior's hand, bringing joy and purpose to families (Psalm 127:3-5). Scripture outlines mutual duties for parents and children, emphasizing love, instruction, discipline, and modeling godliness.

### Parents' Responsibilities
- **Train and instruct diligently**: Parents must teach children God's Word and ways throughout daily life—at home, on the road, at bedtime, and rising (Deuteronomy 6:6-9). "Train up a child in the way he should go; even when he is old he will not depart from it" (Proverbs 22:6).
- **Discipline with love**: Loving correction shapes character and prevents folly. "Whoever spares the rod hates his son, but he who loves him is diligent to discipline him" (Proverbs 13:24); "The rod and reproof give wisdom, but a child left to himself brings shame to his mother" (Proverbs 29:15). Discipline aims to restore, not provoke (Hebrews 12:5-11).
- **Nurture without exasperation**: Fathers (and mothers) should bring children up in the discipline and instruction of the Lord, avoiding harshness that discourages (Ephesians 6:4; Colossians 3:21).
- **Model faith**: Live out obedience to God, as Timothy's grandmother Lois and mother Eunice did, passing on sincere faith (2 Timothy 1:5).

### Children's Responsibilities
Children are called to honor and obey parents as part of God's design, with promises of long life and blessing (Exodus 20:12; Ephesians 6:1-3; Colossians 3:20). Jesus Himself submitted to Mary and Joseph (Luke 2:51).

These principles apply in every context, relying on God's grace through prayer, Scripture, and the church community (Philippians 1:6). No parent is perfect, but God equips those who seek Him (James 1:5). If you're a parent navigating challenges, lean on a local church for support and wise counsel. Who was Jeremiah in the Bible?

Jeremiah was a major prophet of the Lord God who ministered to the southern kingdom of Judah during a turbulent period spanning the late 7th and early 6th centuries BC. He was born in Anathoth, a priestly village near Jerusalem, as the son of Hilkiah the priest (Jeremiah 1:1). God called him to prophetic ministry while he was still a young man, even before he was formed in the womb, appointing him as a prophet to the nations (Jeremiah 1:4-5).

His ministry began in the 13th year of King Josiah (around 627 BC) and continued through the reigns of Jehoiakim, Jehoiachin, and Zedekiah, right up to the fall of Jerusalem to Babylon in 586 BC and the early exile (Jeremiah 1:2-3). Jeremiah faithfully warned Judah of God's impending judgment due to their idolatry, covenant unfaithfulness, and refusal to repent, often using symbolic acts like buying a field as a sign of future hope (Jeremiah 32:6-15). Despite intense persecution—including imprisonment, threats, and rejection by his own people—he remained obedient, earning the title "the weeping prophet" for his deep sorrow over Jerusalem's fate (Jeremiah 9:1).

The Book of Jeremiah records his prophecies, including calls to repentance, oracles against nations, and promises of restoration through a new covenant written on the heart (Jeremiah 31:31-34). Tradition attributes the Book of Lamentations to him, a poetic outpouring of grief over Jerusalem's destruction (Lamentations 1:1-2). Jeremiah's life exemplifies faithful endurance amid suffering, pointing ultimately to the hope fulfilled in Christ, who established the new covenant through His blood (Hebrews 8:6-13).
Why did Jesus allow the demons to enter the herd of pigs?

Jesus' encounter with the demon-possessed man in the region of the Gerasenes (Mark 5:1-20; Matthew 8:28-34; Luke 8:26-39) powerfully displays His divine authority over evil spirits, while revealing His compassion for the tormented individual. The demons begged Jesus not to send them into the abyss but instead pleaded to enter a nearby herd of about two thousand pigs (Mark 5:12). Jesus permitted it, and the pigs rushed down the steep bank into the sea and drowned.

Several key reasons emerge from the account:

First, it provided undeniable evidence of the exorcism's reality. The pigs' violent destruction visibly confirmed that the demons had left the man, who was now clothed, in his right mind, and sitting peaceably at Jesus' feet (Mark 5:15; Luke 8:35). This miracle authenticated Jesus' power to the onlookers.

Second, it exposed the destructive nature of demons. Unclean spirits bring chaos and death wherever they go (John 10:10), and their immediate effect on the pigs underscored their malevolence, contrasting with Jesus' life-giving ministry.

Third, Jesus exercised sovereign authority. The demons recognized Him as the "Son of the Most High God" and could not act without His permission (Mark 5:7, 10, 13). He granted their request not out of weakness but to fulfill His purposes, much like His authority over all creation (Colossians 1:16-17).

The setting in a Gentile region (where pig farming was common, unlike Jewish dietary laws in Leviticus 11:7-8) added layers: the pigs symbolized ritual uncleanness, fitting for demons, and the economic loss led the locals to beg Jesus to leave (Mark 5:17). Yet the healed man became an evangelist, proclaiming Jesus' works throughout the Decapolis (Mark 5:19-20).

Ultimately, this event magnifies Jesus as the compassionate Son of God who conquers darkness, inviting us to trust in His unrivaled power for deliverance today (Hebrews 2:14-15).
